subtopic_detail: Iron deficiency anaemia in toddler with excessive cow milk intake
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 22-month-old boy whose mother reports he tires easily on walks and is increasingly pale. He drinks about 750 mL of full-cream cow milk a day from a bottle and refuses meat, eggs and fortified cereal. He was exclusively breastfed until 10 months. Vaccinations are up to date. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He has no overt bleeding, no jaundice and no chronic illness. The blood film shows microcytic hypochromic red cells with anisocytosis.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 116, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air, weight on 50th centile
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival and palmar pallor; cardiovascular and abdominal examination otherwise normal; no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84 g/L, MCV 64 fL, ferritin 6 micrograms/L, CRP 2 mg/L, reticulocytes low

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Trial a multivitamin syrup for six weeks before considering targeted iron treatment
options.rationale: Multivitamin syrups contain too little elemental iron to correct established deficiency anaemia; a therapeutic iron dose is needed alongside dietary change. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Refer to paediatric haematology for an inpatient intravenous iron infusion
options.rationale: IV iron is reserved for failure of oral therapy, malabsorption or intolerance; uncomplicated nutritional deficiency responds to oral iron and dietary modification. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg once daily and restrict cow milk to under 500 mL daily
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron for at least 3 months in nutritional iron deficiency, plus cow milk volume below 500 mL to reduce iron antagonism. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Send haemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude thalassaemia before commencing treatment
options.rationale: The very low ferritin and dietary history give an unambiguous nutritional diagnosis; electrophoresis is reserved if the anaemia fails to respond to iron replacement. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

options.text: Increase dietary iron alone and recheck the full blood count in eight weeks
options.rationale: Diet alone is too slow to correct symptomatic anaemia with Hb 84 g/L; oral iron replacement is required in addition to dietary advice. (Source: RCH CPG Iron deficiency)

explanation.summary: Excessive cow milk intake above 500 mL per day is a leading cause of toddler iron deficiency in Australia because milk displaces iron-rich foods and reduces non-haem iron absorption. The Royal Children's Hospital guideline recommends 3 mg/kg/day of elemental iron orally for at least 3 months, then a further 3 months once Hb normalises to replenish stores. A FBC and ferritin check at 4 to 6 weeks documents response. Dietary modification including red meat, iron-fortified cereals and vitamin C with meals is essential alongside supplementation.
key_points: Cow milk above 500 mL daily is a key dietary risk factor.
Elemental iron dose is 3 mg/kg once daily for three months.
Recheck FBC and ferritin at four to six weeks for response.
IV iron is reserved for oral failure, intolerance or malabsorption.
explanation.pearls: Expect a reticulocyte rise within 5 to 7 days and a Hb rise of about 10 g/L per month on oral iron; failure to respond after 4 weeks should prompt review of adherence, ongoing milk excess, or alternative diagnoses such as coeliac disease.
